slug: Santa Marta summit 'historic' pledge vs. $9.75B new LNG FID
title: Santa Marta summit 'historic' pledge vs. $9.75B new LNG FID — 2026-05-18
content:
The Santa Marta conference (April 24-29, 2026) — the first International Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels — was hailed by 57 participating countries as a "historic" shift from ambition to action. Colombia's minister declared "fossil fuels bring death." The Netherlands framed transition as an economic and security imperative. The public narrative: the world is finally moving from fossil fuel rhetoric to concrete phase-out roadmaps.

## What the Data Says

- Simultaneously: Commonwealth LNG in Louisiana took Final Investment Decision on $9.75 billion export terminal — backed by Mubadala Energy, Kimmeridge, CPP Investments
- The Santa Marta summit produced NO binding targets, NO mandatory timelines, NO enforcement mechanisms — explicitly a "non-negotiation forum"
- ARENA (Australia) hydrogen "Headstart" program simultaneously funding predominantly grey/blue hydrogen (gas-derived)
- Santa Marta workstreams are voluntary — countries "invited" via email to report fossil fuel subsidies
- Oil prices surged 3%+ the same week as the conference closed, driven by Hormuz war risk
- Santa Marta's own Action Insights report called for halting "all new fossil-fuel expansion" — which governments ignored
- 1.4C warming above pre-industrial baseline cited by NASA/Copernicus as "early 2026 estimate"

## The Real Story

Headline: "Fossil Fuel 'Transition': The World Summit and the $9.75 Billion Contradiction" — frame the Santa Marta outcomes against the simultaneous $9.75B LNG FID. Track rhetorical escalation ("fossil fuels = death") against capital flows ($9.75B new LNG infrastructure). The disconnect between the language of urgency and the reality of new fossil infrastructure investment is the story.

Key contrast: "They called for an end to new fossil fuel expansion. Then signed off on $9.75 billion for new LNG."
